inlineContent.text: Autocomplete is an important part of the typing experience, where word suggestions can be presented in a toolbar above the keyboard as the user types, and incorrectly text can be autocorrected.
In KeyboardKit, an
can provide suggestions and predictions to the main
. Unlike other services, there’s no standard service implementation in the open-source SDK.
unlocks a
and a
, next character prediction, next word prediction, etc. Information about Pro features can be found further down.
KeyboardKit has an
namespace with autocomplete-related types, like a
model, a
view, etc.
KeyboardKit has an
that provides observable autocomplete state, and auto-persisted
that can be used to customize the autocomplete behavior.
KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of this class, injects it into
and updates it whenever autocomplete is performed.
In KeyboardKit, an
can provide suggestions when the user types and the text input cursor moves in the text.
KeyboardKit doesn’t have a standard autocomplete service, as it has for other service types. Instead, it injects a
service into
until you set up
or inject your own service implementation. Read more further down.
KeyboardKit has an
-specific
type with auto-persisted settings.
is used by default within KeyboardKit.
The
namespace has autocomplete-specific views, that can be used to mimic native autocomplete toolbars and items.
The autocomplete
can be used to show autocomplete suggestions as the user types.
The view can be styled with a
, which is applied with the
modifier. It can also use completely custom views.
This view is used as the standard toolbar by the
. This means you can style it with a global style modifier.
unlocks a
and a
, and injects a local service into
when you set up KeyboardKit Pro with valid license. You can inherit both services and customize them further.
The
performs local on-device autocomplete. It supports many locales, works offline, doesn’t require Full Access and can integrate with system services, like the local lexicon.
The service supports:

arabic, bulgarian, czech, danish, dutch, dutch_belgium, english, english_gb, english_us, filipino, finnish, french, french_belgium, french_switzerland, german, german_austria, german_switzerland, greek, hebrew, hungarian, irish, italian, norwegian, polish, portuguese_brazil, portuguese, romanian, russian, spanish, swedish, turkish, ukrainian
You can inject a custom
into the service to make it perform next word predictions with an external AI-based service. See the next word prediction section further down for more information.
The
can be used to perform autocomplete by integrating with any external API. It requires that the device is online, and requires Full Access to allow the keyboard to make network requests.
You create a remote autocomplete service by defining the API URL, any potential headers or query parameters, as well as a result type.
The
and
perform next-character predictions on their results.
The next character prediction uses the current autocomplete suggestions to predict which keys that are more likely to be tapped next. This information is used by
to slightly increase the tap area of more probable keys, to reduce the risk of typing error.

Apple’s on-device text prediction utilities stopped supporting next word prediction in iOS 16. KeyboardKit Pro therefore unlocks ways to let the local autocomplete service perform next word prediction via 3rd party AI services.
The easiest way to enable next word prediction, is to add a
to your
configuration:
You can use these pre-defined requests to integrate with Claude or OpenAI, by providing an API key and any optional customizations:
You can also let your users select which next word prediction request to use, with their own private API key. Read more further down.

AI-based next word prediction requires Full Access and is disabled by default, to avoid sending user text to 3rd parties without the user’s explicit consent. Make sure to first ask the user, after which you can enable it with
The
will automatically call
whenever the keyboard text changes, then update the
with the result from the
You can configure the
and override
‘s autocomplete properties and functions to customize the keyboard’s autocomplete behavior.
The
will automatically add an
that lists the autocomplete context
and also gives the keyboard some additional top space for callouts to render without being clipped.
You can create a custom autocomplete service to customize the standard autocomplete behavior or integrate with 3rd party services. You can implement
from scratch, or inherit and customize any
service, like this:
To use this service instead of the standard one, just inject it into
by replacing its
property:
This will make KeyboardKit use your custom autocomplete service instead of the standard one.

inlineContent.text: You can let your users pick a
and specify a
to let them use their own API key and pay for their own consumption.
The
settings will then provide you with a properly configured
, which you can add to your
or
The KeyboardKit Pro
has a visibility setting to show a next word prediction section, which is hidden by default.
